text: The extent to which parties appear to be responsive to citizens in representative democracies is contested. Often studies focus exclusively on the concept of position congruence, missing the importance of understanding voter issue salience. Using data from election manifestos of political parties and the policy preferences of citizens derived from voters surveys (through the Comparative Study of Electoral Systems and various national election studies) in eighteen European countries, covering sixty-three elections between 1972 and 2011 (via the Comparative Manifestos Project, CMP), the paper explores whether the parties react to voters’ priorities in different respects. We find, on the basis of our analyses of the degree of alignment between party and voter concerns, that parties respond to citizens through their election manifestos but in different respects, according to party type: larger parties in particular are concerned with voters’ preference of policy issues while government parties are concerned less with voters’ prioritisation of policy issues and more with other types of issue priorities, and that smaller parties respond more strongly in their preferred issue area but react less to citizens in other respects. These conclusions are of considerable import for the debates in political science relating to political representation and democratic linkages.
